coredata.dc:title: Competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on FRET between core-shell graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles for ultra-sensitive detection of ochratoxin A
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Qingjie
Ke, Chenxi
Wu, Yu
Song, Zichen
Li, Mengjiao
Zhu, Hongda
Guo, Huiling
Sun, Hongmei
Liu, Mingxing
coredata.dc:description: Ochratoxin A (OTA) is a kind of mycotoxin, and developing a sensitive and simple method to detect OTA is of great significance for ensuring food safety and human health. In this study, a competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was designed for the ultra-sensitive detection of OTA. Here, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites were successfully designed, fabricated, and characterized by heteroatom co-doping and metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) techniques, which can improve the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield. This study indicated that FRET between OTA antigen-modified Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs (Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen) and OTA antibody-modified AuNPs can effectively quench the strong fluorescence of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen. The competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on FRET can detect OTA with a linear range of 0.5–1000 pg/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.094 pg/mL under optimal conditions. The recoveries ranged from 94% to 110% for the red wine sample, suggesting that the developed FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor can be an effective tool for the ultra-sensitive detection of OTA, meanwhile, it might provide a better method for the detection of other toxic small molecules in food and the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Competitive fluorescent immunosensor
FRET
Ultra-sensitive detection
MEF
OTA

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs)
Conjugation of Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs with OTA-Antigen
Synthesis of AuNPs and conjugation of AuNPs with Anti-OTA
Principle of FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor for detection of OTA
Optimal conditions for the reaction between Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA-AuNPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei University of Technology Graduate Research Innovation Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Professor Xincai Xiao from South-central Minzu University for his help in the TEM measurements. This work was supported by the Hubei University of Technology Graduate Research Innovation Project ( 2022034 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The principal diagram of the preparation of (A) Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites and (B) FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor.
TEM images of (A) GO, (B) N, S-GQDs, (C) Ag NPs, (D) Ag@SiO2 NPs, and (E) Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. (F) SEM image of AuNPs.
N, S-GQDs and Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs fluorescence spectra with the addition of different volumes of TEOS: (a) N, S-GQDs, and (b-e) influenced by 0.5 μL, 2 μL, 4 μL, and 8 μL TEOS.
(A) Survey scan XPS spectra for Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. (B-F) High-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, S 2p, Ag 3d, and Si 2p XPS spectra of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs, respectively.

(A) The fluorescence spectrum of (a) Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs and (b) Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen. (B) Spectra overlapping between (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs and (b) fluorescence spectrum of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA-AuNPs complexes with different concentrations: the concentrations of Anti-OTA-AuNPs from a to f were 0, 10, 12.5, 20, 25, and 50 μg/mL (based on the calculated concentration of AuNPs).

(A) Linear relationship between OTA concentration and fluorescence intensity. The concentrations of OTA were 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 100, 500, and 1000 pg/mL. (B) The reproducibility of the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor to detect OTA. (C) The specificity of the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor in detecting OTA was evaluated by comparing the effects of OTA and interfering materials on fluorescence signals. All material concentrations were the same (100 ng/mL). (D) At 4 °C, the prepared sensor was kept for 30 days to assess the stability of the method to detect OTA (n = 3).
Comparison of the current method with those previously reported for detection of OTA.
Recovery of different concentrations of OTA in red wine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on FRET between core-shell graphene quantum dots and Au nanoparticles for ultra-sensitive detection of ochratoxin A

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ochratoxin A (OTA) is a kind of mycotoxin, and developing a sensitive and simple method to detect OTA is of great significance for ensuring food safety and human health. In this study, a competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was designed for the ultra-sensitive detection of OTA. Here, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites were successfully designed, fabricated, and characterized by heteroatom co-doping and metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) techniques, which can improve the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield. This study indicated that FRET between OTA antigen-modified Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs (Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen) and OTA antibody-modified AuNPs can effectively quench the strong fluorescence of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen. The competitive fluorescent immunosensor based on FRET can detect OTA with a linear range of 0.5–1000 pg/mL and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.094 pg/mL under optimal conditions. The recoveries ranged from 94% to 110% for the red wine sample, suggesting that the developed FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor can be an effective tool for the ultra-sensitive detection of OTA, meanwhile, it might provide a better method for the detection of other toxic small molecules in food and the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A mycotoxin known as ochratoxin A (OTA) is mostly generated by the genera Aspergillus and Penicillium and is often found in coffee, milk, wine, flavorings, grains, crops, and their by-products [1]. OTA is structurally stable and cannot be degraded by high-temperature treatment, so OTA remains in food products even after the industrial processing of secondary products [2]. Extensive studies have shown that OTA has adverse effects on human and animal health, resulting in neurotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, teratogenicity, and immunosuppression [3,4], and the International Agency for Research on Cancer also considers OTA to be a potential human carcinogen [5,6]. Considering the risk of OTA to human health, some countries have set limits on levels of OTA in beverage and food sources (ranging from 1 to 20 ppb), depending on the type and quality of the particular food [7]. Therefore, close monitoring and effective control of OTA levels are essential for food safety and human health.

Until now, the analytical methods used for OTA quantification include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), thin layer chromatography, electrochemical methods, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7,8]. However, some analytical techniques are of low sensitivity and require expensive equipment and complex and time-consuming procedures, limiting their widespread use in detecting OTA in food safety [9]. Based on the interaction between antigen and antibody, immunosensors have attracted wide attention due to their simple operation, high specificity, and sensitivity [10–12]. Optical detection has also proved to be one of the most commodious techniques, in which the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system has fine sensitivity and selectivity. FRET is a non-radiative electron-excited energy transfer process that occurs between the excited donor and the energy acceptor [13,14]. FRET-based immunosensors do not require any coating or washing steps and are a dissociation-free homogeneous immunoassay that enables one-step detection [15,16] and has been increasingly used for food analysis in recent years. The selection of suitable donor-acceptor pairs is particularly important in FRET-based immunoassays. AuNPs could be considered a superior energy acceptor in the FRET system due to their high extinction coefficient and wide absorption spectrum, which can achieve a strong quenching effect on the donor [17,18].

In recent years, fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted more and more attention [19,20], among which graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel fluorescent nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm [21]. GQDs have the advantages of stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, and simple synthesis, GQDs as the promising "green" fluorescent probes for constructing FRET-based immunosensors [22,23]. However, the relatively low fluorescence intensity and quantum yield (QY) of GQDs have been a hindrance to their adoption. It is found that element doping can improve the photoelectric properties of GQDs, especially nitrogen (N) or sulfur (S) doping in GQDs is an effective way to improve its QY [24–26]. Moreover, metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) is also widely used to improve quantum yield, which can enhance the intensity of light radiation by generating plasmon resonance of metal particles [27]. It was reported that silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are a kind of metal with MEF function and high enhancement efficiency [28] and SiO2 is used to regulate the distance between the fluorescent substance and the metal [29], whose combination can realize sometimes of fluorescence improvement. Therefore, heteroatomic co-doping can be combined with MEF to improve the quantum yield of GQDs and enhance the fluorescence signal, and the modified GQDs can be used as fluorescence donors in the FRET system.

In this work, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites were successfully prepared by freely assembling the N and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) on the surface of the prepared Ag@SiO2-NH2 NPs ( Fig. 1A) and characterized. Subsequently, a FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor was constructed for the detection of OTA. In this immunosensor, OTA antigen-modified Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs (Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen) was used as donor fluorescence and AuNPs-labeled OTA antibodies (Anti-OTA-AuNPs) were used as a quencher. Due to the immune reaction between OTA antigen and OTA antibody, the fluorescence of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen was quenched in the presence of Anti-OTA-AuNPs. After OTA was added to the Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen-Anti-OTA-AuNPs system, Anti-OTA-AuNPs separated from the Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen surface and attached to OTA due to competitive immune response, restoring the fluorescence. Combining fluorescence resonance energy transfer with competitive immunoassay allows the entire testing process to be completed quickly without various time-consuming incubation, separation, and cleaning steps. The spiked recoveries of OTA in red wine samples were also investigated. This FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor might also provide a useful tool for the detection of other mycotoxins.

Concentrated sulfuric acid, nitric acid, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), hydrogen peroxide (15%), ammonia (25–28%), glucose, and trisodium citrate were acquired from Sinopharm Holding Chemical Reagents Co., LTD. Graphite powder and (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxylsilane (APTES) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). L-cysteine (L-Cys) was purchased from Amadas Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate was obtained from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was supplied by Beijing Biotechnology Co., LTD (Beijing, China). Ochratoxin A antigen (OTA-Angen), ochratoxin A antibody (Anti-OTA), and ochratoxin A standard (OTA) were purchased from Shenzhen Anti Biotechnology Co., LTD (Shenzhen, China). Red wine was purchased from Tmall supermarket. Ultrapure water from a water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm, Bedford Millipore, Ma, USA) was used in all experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum was measured with a spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, Perkin Elmer, USA). The fluorescence spectra and intensity were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS-55, Perkin Elmer, USA). The Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy was acquired from the FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker IFS 55, Faellanden, Switzerland). The morphology of nanomaterials was observed by transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100 F, Hitachi, Japan) and scanning electron microscope (SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). The elemental content was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (VG Multilab 2000, Thermo, USA).

The mixture of 250 mg NaNO3, 500 mg graphite powder, and 15 mL concentrated sulfuric acid was stirred for 0.5 h under the condition of an ice bath, and then 2 g KMnO4 was slowly added to the suspension below 20 ℃. Waiting for the temperature of the reaction was raised to 37 ℃, the obtained mixture was diluted by adding 30 mL ultrapure water, and the suspension was stirred for another 1 h. After the above mixture was reacted at 90 ℃ for 1 h, 20 mL water and 15 mL 30% (w/v) hydrogen peroxide was added to terminate the reaction. The prepared GO was washed with 5% hydrochloric acid and ultrapure water several times and freeze-dried for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N, S-GQDs were prepared according to the previous literature [30]. 30 mg L-cysteine and 10 mg graphene oxide (GO) powder were ultrasonically mixed with 10 mL ultrapure water for 30 min, and then 100 μL ammonia was added to adjust pH to 8–9. The suspension was put into the autoclave that had a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) liner and fried for six hours at 200 °C. Once it had warmed to room temperature, the large particles were filtered out with a 0.22 µm Microporous filter, and the supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the obtained N, S-GQDs were freeze-dried for 24 h for further experiments.

Ag NPs were synthesized based on the procedure described in the study [31]. Briefly, 4 mL of 1% trisodium citrate solution was added to the mixture of 20 mg AgNO3 and 100 mL ultra-pure water, and the reaction was carried out at 95 ℃ for 1 h to ensure complete reduction. The obtained Ag NPs were dispersed into 10 mL ultrapure water after washing several times with water.

Ag@SiO2 NPs were prepared to refer to the report [32]. After 8 mL ethanol, 1 mL water and 100 μL NH3·H2O were added to 1 mL Ag NPs with stirring, different amounts of TEOS (0.5, 2, 4, and 8 μL) were added at 25 ℃ for 6 h. The Ag@SiO2 NPs were dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water after washing sometimes with ultrapure water and ethanol.

Ag@SiO2-NH2 NPs were prepared by adding 20 μL APTES to 10 mL Ag@SiO2 NPs solution at 25 ℃ for 12 h. The obtained Ag@SiO2-NH2 NPs were dispersed into 10 mL ultrapure water after they were cleaned several times with water.

3 mL ultrapure water and 100 μL Ag@SiO2-NH2 NPs solution were added to 50 μL of 1 mg/mL N, S-GQDs solution, and the mixture was stirred at 25 ℃ for 12 h. After the above suspension was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites were acquired by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs with OTA-Antigen
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mL of 1 mg/mL Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs solution with the addition of 10 μL of 10 mg/mL EDC was stirred at 25 °C for 5 min to activate its free carboxylic acid groups. Next, 10 μL of 10 mg/mL NHS was added to the mixture, and the mixture was stirred at 37 °C for 12 h. Then, 50 μL of 10 μg/mL OTA-Antigen was given to the system, and it reacted for two hours in the dark at 37 °C. Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen was obtained, dispersed with PBS, and kept at 4 °C and in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs and conjugation of AuNPs with Anti-OTA
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs was synthesized based on the procedure described in the study [33]. Briefly, 4 mL of 1% trisodium citrate solution was added after 100 mL of HAuCl4 at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL had boiled at 95 °C. The 50 μg/mL concentration of AuNPs that was obtained was kept at 4 °C in the dark.

The pH value of AuNPs was adjusted to 7–8 with 0.1 mol/L K2CO3 or 0.1 mmol/L HCl. 100 μL of 10 μg/mL Anti-OTA antibody was added to 4 mL AuNPs and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 2 h to obtain Anti-OTA-AuNPs nanoprobe.

In a 2 mL tube, 1.0 mL Anti-OTA-AuNPs solution and 25 μL PBS (pH 7.4) or various amounts of OTA (0.5, 1, 5, 10, 100, 500, 1000 pg/mL) were added, thoroughly mixed and reacted at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 50 μL Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen solution was added and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The fluorescence spectra and intensity were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 320 nm excitation wavelength (Ex). In the presence and absence of OTA, the fluorescence intensities of the conjugate and blank were measured and recorded as F and F0, respectively. By deducting the fluorescence intensity of the blank from the fluorescence intensity of the conjugate, the change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) was calculated. ΔF and OTA concentrations were used to quantitatively detect OTA.

For the detection of real samples, first of all, the commercially available red wine was diluted to 3% with PBS buffer, and 2.5 pg/mL, 25 pg/mL, and 100 pg/mL OTA were added into samples according to the standard addition method for the recovery test. The fluorescence intensity was measured according to the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor, and the OTA concentration was calculated by substituting the results into the established standard curve. The feasibility of this method was evaluated by comparing it with the OTA scalar and calculating the recovery rate.

TEM and SEM were used to study the morphology and structure of GO, N, S-GQDs, Ag NPs, Ag@SiO2 NPs, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs and AuNPs. As can be seen from Fig. 2A, GO was thin and transparent with a crumpled lamellar structure, similar to the typical structure of graphene [34]. Fig. 2B provided a TEM image of prepared N, S-GQDs. N, S-GQDs exhibited a spherical morphology with a uniform size distribution and an average diameter of 4 nm. As shown in Fig. 2C, Ag NPs had a spherical structure with an average diameter of 60 nm and showed clear profiles in the solution without obvious aggregation. Fig. 2D depicted the TEM images of Ag@SiO2 NPs with 4 μL of TEOS, and the thickness was measured to be 23 nm which satisfied the requirement of fluorescence enhancement and can be used for our further research [35]. Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposite was depicted in Fig. 2E using a TEM image, and N, S-GQDs were self-assembled around the surface of the Ag@SiO2 NPs core-shell. As demonstrated in Fig. 2F, AuNPs were spherical and around 35 nm in size. FT-IR was used to study the functional groups of GO and N, S-GQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, both GO and N, S-GQDs showed aromatic CC skeleton vibration bands near 1620 cm−1, which proved the existence of graphite structure [34]. There were two bands at 3386 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 caused by the stretching vibrations of O-H and CO, indicating that GO was successfully prepared and had a large number of oxygen-containing groups on its surface. In addition, N, S-GQDs showed a new absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of C-N. To sum up, the N, S-GQDs contained a large of hydrophilic groups, such as -OH and -COOH, which could greatly improve the water solubility.

The optical properties of GO and N, S-GQDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. It could be seen from Fig. S2A that GO had an absorption peak at 232 nm, while N, S-GQDs had an absorption peak at 317 nm due to n − π * electron transition, which was similar to the reported GQDs [36]. As shown in Fig. S2B, there was no fluorescence in GO aqueous solution, while N, S-GQDs showed a maximum emission peak of 396 nm at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Moreover, the presence of C, N, S, and O in the XPS measurement spectrum further proved the successful synthesis of N, S-GQD (Fig. S2C).

To investigate the fluorescence enhancement effect of Ag@SiO2-NH2 NPs on N, S-GQDs, the fluorescence intensities of the original N, S-GQDs and four different Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQD composites were measured. Fig. 3 depicted how Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs' fluorescence spectra and emission peaks were identical to those of N, S-GQDs, N, S-GQDs were the only source of fluorescence in the composite system. The four nanoparticles in Fig. 3 had greater fluorescence relative to N, S-GQDs, and the enhancement effect first grew and subsequently reduced as the degree of TEOS supplementation was raised (0.5–8.0 μL). The best fluorescence enhancement impact on N, S-GQDs was achieved with the addition of 4 μL TEOS (the thickness of SiO2 was 23 nm), and the fluorescence intensity could be doubled. According to the research, the amount of TEOS could affect the thickness of the SiO2 shell on the surface of Ag NPs [35]. Combined with the above analysis results, it can be concluded that the SiO2 shell thickness plays an important role in this experiment, and the synthesized Ag@SiO2 NPs can be used to regulate the fluorescence intensity of N, S-GQDs.

The XPS spectra of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs were provided in Fig. 4. The total XPS spectra showed six peaks at 284 eV, 531 eV, 398 eV, 198 eV, 367 eV, and 102 eV, indicating that the composite was composed of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), silver (Ag) and silicon (Si). The C 1 s XPS high resolution spectrum had three diffraction peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.8 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of CC, C-S, and C-N&CO, respectively [37]. There were two peaks in the high resolution spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 4 C). The peak at 398.2 eV could be attributed to pyridine N, and the peak at 401.3 eV corresponded to graphite N [38]. Notably, since the peak strength of pyridine N was stronger than that of graphite N, it can be assumed that pyridine N predominated in Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. The presence of sulfur oxide and C-S bonds was confirmed by two main peaks at 163.3 eV and 168.5 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p. The Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 spin-orbit coupling was responsible for the two diffraction peaks at 367.75 eV and 373.75 eV in the Ag 3d high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 4E. The presence of Si in composite nanomaterials was demonstrated by the XPS binding energy of Si 2p spectrum, which peaked at 102.35 eV (Fig. 4 F) and was comparable to the binding energy of Si-N [39]. According to the above obtained results, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites were effectively created.

Quinoline sulfate was used as the reference for measuring and calculating the QY of N, S-GQDs and Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. The following equation was used to compute the quantum yield:
(1)
φ
n
= φ
r
× (I
n
×A
r
×η
n
2
)/(I
r
×A
n
×η
r
2
)
Where the QY value, the fluorescence peak regions, the absorbance intensity, and the refractive index are represented by φ, I, A, and η, respectively. Nanomaterials and the reference are denoted by the subscripts "n" and "r" in this instance. According to earlier investigations, the quantum yields of N, S-GQDs and Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs are 26.8% and 34.9% [40], respectively, showing that Ag@SiO2 NPs can increase the quantum yield of N, S-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor for detection of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1B showed a schematic of the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor for OTA detection. With the coupling of EDC and NHS, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs can be combined with OTA antigen (OTA-Antigen) through an amide bond to get Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen. After conjugation, the fluorescence spectrum of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen had little change compared with Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs ( Fig. 5 A). The amino group on OTA antibody can also be covalently conjugated with AuNPs to obtain Anti-OTA-AuNPs.

In the absence of target OTA, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen-Anti-OTA-AuNPs immune complexes were formed between Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA-AuNPs under the immune recognition of antigens and antibodies. The donor Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs emission spectrum and the acceptor AuNPs absorption spectra had some overlap, as shown in Fig. 5B, which provided a solid platform for energy transfer. Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen-Anti-OTA-AuNPs immune complexes closed the distance between Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs and AuNPs, and FRET occurred when the distance between them was less than 10 nm [41]. The energy of excited Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs was transmitted to near AuNPs via dipole-dipole interaction, and the fluorescence of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs was quenched by AuNPs.

However, in the presence of target OTA and Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen would compete with anti-OTA-AuNPs. With the increase of OTA concentration in the sample, the probability of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen binding with Anti-OTA-AuNPs decreased, thus banning FRET between them and reducing the effect of AuNPs on Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs fluorescence quenching. In other words, the fluorescence intensity increased with the OTA concentration, successfully converting the OTA concentration into a fluorescence signal. Thus, we established a sensitive and selective method for the detection of OTA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for the reaction between Ag@SiO2@N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Various concentrations of energy acceptors can influence the fluorescence resonance energy transfer system. In this study, the concentration of receptor Anti-OTA-AuNPs was examined. After adding Anti-OTA-AuNPs to Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen, Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA-AuNPs will be close to each other through immune action, resulting in FRET, which will reduce the fluorescence intensity of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen. As shown in Fig. 5 C, with the concentration of energy receptor Anti-OTA-AuNPs increasing from 0 to 50 g/mL, the fluorescence intensity of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen continued to decrease. To get a great transfer effect and fluorescence intensity together, Anti-OTA-AuNPs of 25 μg/mL were selected in this study. Here, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Eq) was calculated as 0.45. Eq was calculated by the equation given in the reference as follows [13], where F0 and Fq are the fluorescence intensity of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen in the absence and presence of Anti-OTA-AuNPs.
(2) Eq = (F0-Fq)/F0

Experimental factors, such as the incubation temperature and time of OTA, are necessary to get a high-response fluorescence sensor. Considering the stability of the Anti-OTA structure, the incubation temperature was varied from 4 ℃ to 50 ℃. As the temperature increased from 4 ℃ to 50 ℃, the change of fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased and reached the maximum value at 37 ℃ (Fig. S4A), which may be due to the better bioactivity and sensitivity of Anti-OTA at this temperature. Therefore, the incubation temperature of OTA and Anti-OTA was selected at 37 ℃.

Fig. S4B showed the influence of incubation time on detection. With the increase in reaction time, the change in fluorescence intensity of the system gradually increased. After the addition of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen, free OTA, and Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen were competitively bound to Anti-OTA-AuNPs via antigen-antibody interaction. The reaction reached equilibrium when the incubation period was longer than 120 min since there was no discernible rise in fluorescence intensity. As a result, the experimental condition for this study was set to be 120 min

According to the above procedure, under ideal circumstances, the fluorescence intensity of the system at various OTA concentrations was measured, and a calibration curve was built based on the correlation between OTA concentration (C) and the corresponding change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF). With the increase of OTA concentration in the sample, the probability of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA combination decreased. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effect of AuNPs on Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs was minimized, and the fluorescence intensity of the system was improved, which successfully converted the concentration of OTA into a fluorescence signal. Fig. 6 A showed that the change of fluorescence intensity increased linearly with the logarithm of OTA concentration. The linear range of OTA was 0.5 pg/mL to 1.0 ng/mL, the equation of the standard curve was ΔF = 112.545 COTA + 285.356 (C: pg/mL), and the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.994. The detection limit was calculated as 0.094 pg/mL (S/N = 3). As shown in Table 1, this FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor was more sensitive than most previous methods used to detect OTA, with a wider detection range and lower detection limits. In addition, the method proposed in this study did not require complex operation and expensive instruments, which shortened the time of analysis and detection.

To assess the reproducibility of the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensors for OTA detection, five samples for OTA detection were replicated under the same conditions (Fig. 6B). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the measurements was 2.0%, indicating acceptable reproducibility of the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor.

Food contained amino acids, proteins, sugars, etc., so we chose cysteine (L-Cys), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and glucose to simulate real samples. Selective studies were also conducted on potential interfering substances, such as AFB1, Warfarin, ZEN, Vitamin B1, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, and Mn2+. As shown in Fig. 6 C and Fig. S5, the above substances were co-incubated with Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen to competitively bind Anti-OTA-AuNPs. The results showed that only OTA and the mixture competed for the binding sites of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA, reducing the combination probability of Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen and Anti-OTA and the quenching effect of AuNPs on Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs, while the other substances almost did not cause the change of fluorescence signal. These results indicated that the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor was selective for the detection of OTA and could be used in the presence of other substances.

The stability of the sensor also played an important role in the practicability of the evaluation method. As shown in Fig. 6D, the prepared sensor was evaluated for fluorescence signals in different stages after being placed in a refrigerator set to 4 °C. The sensor's fluorescence value was 95.1% of its initial value after 30 days. The outcomes of the experiment supported that the FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor had good stability in detecting OTA.

To demonstrate the effectiveness of FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensors, OTA content in commercial red wine was detected. 2.5 pg/mL, 25 pg/mL, and 100 pg/mL OTA were added to the red wine sample by standard addition method, and fluorescence signals were analyzed by the immunosensor. As shown in Table 2, the recovery rates of OTA at three different concentrations ranged from 97.96% to 110.36%. The good recovery rate and low relative standard deviation indicate that this FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor has the potential for practical application in OTA detection.

In our study, a FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor was constructed for the ultra-sensitive detection of OTA. Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs nanocomposites and AuNPs were successfully prepared and characterized, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of AuNPs for Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs was 45%. In the established FRET system, the OTA in samples and the donor Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs-OTA-Antigen will combine with the receptor Anti-OTA-AuNPs competitively, thus resulting in the fluorescence intensity recovery from quenching Ag@SiO2 @N, S-GQDs. Under optimal conditions, this FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor had a linear response to OTA in the range of 0.5 pg/mL-1.0 ng/mL with a detection limit of 0.094 pg/mL. This immunoassay had good reproducibility, selectivity, and stability, and showed the recoveries of OTA range from 97.96% to 110.36% for the red wine sample. Notably, the entire assay process can be completed in a short time without the need for multiple time-consuming culture, separation, and washing steps. Because of the excellent performance of FRET and competitive immunoassay, this FRET-based competitive fluorescent immunosensor also offers a novel strategy for the detection of other mycotoxins.

Song Zichen: Data curation, Software. Yu Qingjie: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ke Chenxi: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Wu Yu: Data curation, Software. Liu Mingxing: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Guo Huiling: Investigation, Project administration, Resources. Sun Hongmei: Investigation, Project administration, Resources. Li Mengjiao: Data curation, Software. Zhu Hongda: Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Professor Xincai Xiao from South-central Minzu University for his help in the TEM measurements. This work was supported by the Hubei University of Technology Graduate Research Innovation Project (2022034).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133182.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A SERS-based competitive immunoassay for highly sensitive and specific detection of ochratoxin A
Analyst 14

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ding, H. Shang, X. Wang, L. Chen, A SERS-based competitive immunoassay for highly sensitive and specific detection of ochratoxin A, Analyst 14 (2020) 6079, https://doi.org/10.1039/D0AN01220C.
